ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Ayam Geprek (Indonesian smashed fried chicken). FOODON_03301521 (fried chicken food product) is used as the closest parent. A more specific term for Indonesian geprek-style chicken with sambal bawang does not exist in FoodOn as of April 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Ayam Geprek with sambal. A00LC (Chicken meat, cooked) is used as the closest defensible parent. The sambal bawang component is not captured in this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Ayam Geprek or Indonesian smashed fried chicken with sambal bawang as of April 2026.

preparation_method: dressed with sambal bawang (spicy shallot-chilli relish)

serving.description: 1 serving plate (chicken piece with sambal bawang)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Ayam Geprek Spicy Level 2 consists of one piece of battered and deep-fried chicken (approximately 200 g) smashed and topped with a generous portion of spicy shallot-chilli sambal bawang (approximately 80 g), totalling roughly 280 g per plate.

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken (battered, bone-in) and sambal bawang composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (NDB 05062) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table sambal data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Ayam Geprek with sambal bawang. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite: approximately 72% battered fried chicken thigh (USDA SR Legacy NDB 05062) and 28% sambal bawang (shallot-chilli relish, estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component data for shallots, chillies, and oil). The composite reflects the typical 200 g chicken + 80 g sambal serving ratio scaled to per-100 g. Values are rounded to one decimal place. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of battered fried chicken and mixed spiced dishes — analogous estimate based on fried chicken (battered) GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Geprek with sambal bawang. GI is estimated at 48 (low) based on battered fried chicken analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 40–55 for battered/breaded fried chicken products due to the wheat flour coating. The sambal bawang component (shallots, chillies, oil) contributes negligible glycemic carbohydrates and does not materially raise the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 26.3) / 100 ≈ 12.6, rounded to 11.8 after accounting for fibre adjustment. Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1176 mg per serving) from seasoned batter, sambal, and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Ayam Geprek due to its high sodium load per serving (~1176 mg, approximately 51% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced-salt preparation or consume only a small portion infrequently.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~12 per serving) from wheat flour batter coating; high fat content may impair postprandial insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Ayam Geprek in moderation, pairing it with non-starchy vegetables rather than white rice to limit total glycemic load, and should be mindful of the high fat content which can contribute to insulin resistance over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~3.4 g per 100 g), high sodium (~420 mg per 100 g), and deep-frying oil absorption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Ayam Geprek consumption due to its combination of high saturated fat, high sodium, and significant total fat from deep-frying, all of which are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes; occasional small portions are preferable to regular consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Geprek with Spicy Onion Sambal (Spicy Level 2) is an Indonesian fried chicken dish in which a piece of battered and deep-fried chicken — typically a bone-in thigh or drumstick — is placed on a stone mortar and smashed (geprek) together with a freshly pounded spicy shallot-chilli relish known as sambal bawang. The word 'geprek' derives from Javanese meaning 'to smash' or 'to crush', and the technique integrates the crispy fried chicken with the aromatic, pungent sambal rather than simply topping it. The sambal bawang at Spicy Level 2 contains shallots (bawang merah), garlic (bawang putih), red chillies, and bird's eye chillies (cabe rawit) fried briefly in oil, delivering a garlicky, sharp, moderately spicy flavour profile. The chicken itself is coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter that produces a crunchy exterior, while the interior remains juicy. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 218 kcal, 16.2 g protein, 13.1 g fat (3.4 g saturated), and 9.4 g carbohydrate, with a notable sodium content of ~420 mg per 100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at 48 (low GI) due to the wheat flour batter, but the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~12) when consumed as a full plate. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium and saturated fat, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For people with Type 2 Diabetes, the moderate glycemic load and high fat content warrant portion control and avoidance of pairing with high-GI staples such as white rice. Ayam Geprek originated in Yogyakarta, Java, Indonesia, and has become one of the most popular street food and casual dining dishes across Indonesia and in Indonesian diaspora communities in Singapore and Malaysia. It is typically served with steamed white rice, cucumber slices, and additional sambal on the side.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Ayam Geprek with sambal bawang in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy battered fried chicken thigh data and FAO/INFOODS SEA component-level data for shallots, chillies, and cooking oil. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for battered fried chicken. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates (72% battered fried chicken thigh per USDA SR Legacy NDB 05062, 28% sambal bawang estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component data) scaled to per-100 g. No exact approved-source entry exists for this dish. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for battered fried chicken thigh (NDB 05062) and is not available from the FAO/INFOODS SEA sambal component data used in this composite estimate. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the USDA SR Legacy source and FAO/INFOODS SEA component data; values are plausible but carry medium uncertainty. 4. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for battered fried chicken; no direct GI measurement for Ayam Geprek exists in the approved GI literature. 5. The field dataed in the input (Smile Martabak - Funan) is noted as the source context; cuisine_tags from the input (Stuffed Pancake, Pancake, Coffee) appear to be restaurant-level tags unrelated to this specific dish and have not been carried forward. 6. Halal status is flagged as true based on the Indonesian Muslim-majority culinary context and typical preparation; verification per specific restaurant is recommended. 7. Serving size of 280 g is an estimate based on typical Indonesian geprek chicken portion sizes; actual restaurant portions may vary.
